topics.title: Getting Started
topics.summary: A safe starting path is to use MuseTalk, turn Stream Mode on, keep PocketTTS as the backend, choose a low-VRAM MuseTalk mode, make sure LM Studio is running, then initialize the system.
bullets: Choose Avatar Engine = MuseTalk for the built-in lip-sync avatar pipeline.
Turn Stream Mode on if you want replies to begin speaking sooner.
When Stream Mode is on, the app defaults the TTS backend to PocketTTS.
Choose a MuseTalk VRAM mode based on your hardware. Low VRAM and Very Low VRAM are safer starting points on smaller GPUs.
Make sure LM Studio is running and that a model is visible in the LLM Model list.
Click INITIALIZE SYSTEM to launch the engine.

topics.title: LM Studio
topics.summary: LM Studio provides the language model used by the app. The app expects LM Studio to be running and serving a model locally.
bullets: If the model list is empty or says Scanning / No Models, make sure LM Studio is open.
Load a model in LM Studio so the app can select it.
The selected LLM model affects the assistant's behavior and can also influence streaming feel.

topics.title: Streaming and TTS
topics.summary: Stream Mode and the TTS backend work together to shape responsiveness and expressiveness.
bullets: When Stream Mode is turned on, the app defaults the TTS backend to PocketTTS.
When Stream Mode is turned off, the app defaults back to Chatterbox / TurboTTS.
PocketTTS is usually the faster streaming option.
Chatterbox is usually the more expressive option and may better handle emotional tags like laughs or other expressive sounds.

topics.title: MuseTalk VRAM Modes
topics.summary: MuseTalk VRAM modes trade memory use, GPU workload, and startup/render behavior.
bullets: Quality keeps more work on GPU and is the heaviest mode.
Balanced is a middle-ground mode.
Low VRAM and Very Low VRAM reduce GPU pressure and are safer on smaller cards.
Very Low VRAM is a good first-run compatibility choice.
Different machines can prefer different modes, which is why Dry Run exists.

topics.title: Dry Run
topics.summary: Dry Run profiles the current setup and recommends chunking and startup settings based on measured performance.
bullets: Dry Run is most useful for streaming setups.
During Dry Run, the app can auto-generate follow-up prompts to keep collecting samples.
Dry Run uses a fixed 600-token cap internally so runs stay comparable.
The result can be saved as a Performance Profile.
Dry Run settings are meant to optimize the pipeline, not your persona behavior.

topics.title: Performance Profiles
topics.summary: Performance Profiles store pipeline and chunking settings that can be loaded independently of persona presets.
bullets: Profiles can store stream mode, TTS backend, MuseTalk VRAM mode, and chunking values.
Performance Profiles do not override the normal Brain sampling sliders on load, except model_name remains part of the profile.
The app ships with starter profiles like Recommended Stream, Very Low VRAM Stream, and Expressive Stream.

topics.title: Persona and Brain
topics.summary: Persona and Brain settings shape how the assistant behaves and speaks rather than how the render pipeline performs.
bullets: Persona contains voice selection, expressive tags, and the main system prompt.
PocketTTS is bundled with the app and is used automatically when selected. A PocketTTS interpreter path exists only as an advanced override.
Brain contains sampling settings like temperature, top-p, top-k, repeat penalty, min-p, and optional response length limit.
These are usually persona-style settings rather than performance-tuning settings.

topics.title: Emotional Tags and Voice Tags
topics.summary: The app supports two main kinds of expressive tags: facial-expression tags for the avatar and vocal-action tags for speech.
bullets: Visual mood tags set the avatar expression state: [neutral], [happy], [sad], [angry], [shy], [surprised].
Voice sound tags trigger vocal actions: [laugh], [chuckle], [sigh], [groan], [gasp], [clear throat], [sniff].
Visual mood tags are state-like. They can stay active until another visual mood tag changes the expression.
Voice sound tags are action-like. They are brief expressive events inside speech.
Chatterbox / TurboTTS is usually the better backend for expressive vocal tags and sounds.
PocketTTS is usually the faster streaming backend, but it may not express vocal-action tags as richly as Chatterbox.
Example: [surprised] You did what? [laugh] [surprised] Oh my god, are you okay? [happy] Or just clumsy?
Do not use emojis in spoken output.

topics.title: Avatar Engines
topics.summary: The app supports different avatar engines with different strengths.
bullets: MuseTalk is the built-in lip-sync avatar pipeline and the main target for Dry Run and VRAM tuning.
VSeeFace is the other avatar engine option exposed in the app.
If you want the app's built-in streaming avatar rendering and MuseTalk VRAM modes, choose MuseTalk.

topics.title: Body Tab and Hand Doctor
topics.summary: The Body area is for avatar pose/body shaping and hand calibration tools.
bullets: The Body tab contains sliders for body and pose-related behavior.
Hand Doctor is a utility for adjusting and inspecting hand rotation/calibration behavior.
These controls are separate from LLM behavior and chunking.

topics.title: Dynamics Tab
topics.summary: The Dynamics tab adjusts motion-style parameters such as sway, breath, and other expressive movement controls.
bullets: Use it to change how active or calm the avatar feels.
These are movement/animation controls rather than pipeline performance controls.

topics.title: Chunking
topics.summary: Chunking controls how text is split for speech and avatar rendering, especially in streaming mode.
bullets: Streaming chunking tries to balance early startup with more natural punctuation-aware chunk boundaries.
Dry Run can tune chunking automatically for the current machine.
The Chunking tab is global pipeline tuning, not persona tuning.

topics.title: Tutorials
keywords: how do i learn the app
topics.summary: The app includes a Tutorials tab and can show a first-run tutorial prompt once.
bullets: Getting Started is a guided walkthrough.
First Run is an interactive onboarding tutorial.
Tutorials are loaded from JSON, so more can be added later without hardcoding them into the UI.

topics.title: Presets and Tutorial Persona
topics.summary: Presets store persona-style configuration like prompts, voice settings, and sampling values.
bullets: Tutorial Persona is a bundled preset used by the tutorial flow.
Loading a preset changes persona and brain-related settings rather than performance-profile chunking settings.
Performance Profiles are separate from persona presets.
